🤖 *Automated note from Repo Assist.*

**Heads-up: the bundle targets `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk 18.5.0`, which is unlisted on NuGet.**

Dependabot just opened PR #1193 to bump Test.Sdk directly from `18.4.0` to `18.5.1`. According to the release notes, 18.5.0 was deliberately delisted because of a `System.Collections.Immutable` binding mismatch ([vstest #15718](https://github.com/microsoft/vstest/pull/15718)), and 18.5.1 is the correct release.

**Recommendation:** do **not** use the click-to-create-PR link above as-is for the Test.Sdk part. Instead:

| Dependency | Suggested action |
|---|---|
| FSharp.Core 202 → 203 | Merge Dependabot PR #1180 |
| FSharp.Compiler.Service 202 → 203 | Merge Dependabot PR #1179 |
| System.Text.Json 10.0.6 → 10.0.7 | Merge Dependabot PR #1182 |
| FSharp.Data 8.1.10 → 8.1.11 | Merge Dependabot PR #1183 |
| Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk 18.4.0 → **18.5.1** | Merge Dependabot PR **#1193** (not the bundle) |

The bundle branch cannot be updated to 18.5.1 by Repo Assist (protected file constraint), so merging the individual Dependabot PRs is the recommended path.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Thanks for flagging this, `@rmunn`! A quick clarification on the immediate risk:

**Short-term (after April 24):** The pinned version `1.0.410202` in the `docs/Dockerfile` will **still be installable** after deprecation. NuGet deprecation marks a package as deprecated in the gallery UI but does **not** remove it — `dotnet tool install --global Microsoft.dotnet-interactive --version 1.0.410202` will continue to work. So the Dockerfile won't break on April 24.

**Longer-term concerns** (both real):
1. **The .NET 7.0 SDK Docker image** (`FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:7.0`) has been EOL since May 2024. This is arguably the more urgent issue — the base image may stop receiving security patches from Microsoft.
2. **Compatibility**: Updating to a current .NET SDK (8.0 LTS / 9.0 / 10.0) would require a compatible version of `Microsoft.dotnet-interactive`. The tool's versioning history shows versions targeting .NET 6/7 vs .NET 8. With the tool now deprecated, there may not be a .NET 10-compatible release ever published.

**The Dockerfile is only used for mybinder.org** (the Binder launch badge on the docs), not in CI. So it's important for interactive docs exploration but not for the build pipeline.

**Possible paths forward:**
- Pin a newer `dotnet-interactive` version targeting .NET 8 and update the SDK to 8.0 LTS
- Investigate [Fable REPL]((fable.io/redacted) or other browser-based F# alternatives for the Binder use case
- Consider whether mybinder.org support is still actively used/needed

Happy to open a draft PR to at least update the base image to .NET 8 if that would be helpful — though we'd need to verify dotnet-interactive compatibility first.

Good news — this has been implemented! PR **#1164** adds a `--root` option to `fsdocs watch`, allowing you to override the root URL for Codespaces and reverse-proxy setups. It also fixes the WebSocket hot-reload URL to use `window.location.host` so live reload works correctly regardless of how the page is served.

Please take a look at [#1164](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/pull/1164) and let us know if it meets your needs!

- Adds `--surface-background: var(--header-background)` and `--surface-border: var(--header-border)` to `:root` (fully backwards-compatible defaults)

- Leaves the actual `<header>` element untouched on `--header-*`

Please review when you get a chance!

🤖 *Repo Assist here.* Thanks for the feedback!

The root cause was a **dead zone** between the cursor and tooltip. The tooltip is positioned 20px below the cursor, so when the mouse moves downward from the trigger into that gap, `mouseout` fires but `relatedTarget` is not the tooltip — causing it to hide immediately.

**Fix applied**: instead of hiding synchronously on `mouseout`, I now schedule the hide with a **150ms delay** (via `setTimeout`). If the mouse enters the tooltip (or returns to the trigger) within that window, the hide is cancelled. This is the standard pattern used by most tooltip libraries.

The updated code is now pushed to this PR.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Great question! Here's a breakdown:

### Where it happens

`HtmlElement.ToString()` in [`src/FSharp.Formatting.Common/HtmlModel.fs`](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/blob/main/src/FSharp.Formatting.Common/HtmlModel.fs), in the big `rec format` match expression. Each DU case calls `format "<tag-name>" props children level` to emit the HTML/SVG tag string. Before the fix, both branches passed `"radient"` instead of the correct names.

### How a user hits it

`FSharp.Formatting.HtmlModel` is a **public API** — it's the same DSL used internally to build all fsdocs HTML output. A user would hit this by:

1. Referencing `FSharp.Formatting.Common` in their project
2. Opening `FSharp.Formatting.HtmlModel.Html`
3. Building an SVG element with a gradient fill, e.g.:

```fsharp
open FSharp.Formatting.HtmlModel.Html

let gradientDef =
    defs [] [
        linearGradient [ Id "grad1" ] [
            stop [ Style "stop-color:rgb(255,255,0);stop-opacity:1" ] []
            stop [ Style "stop-color:rgb(255,0,0);stop-opacity:1" ] []
        ]
    ]

let chart =
    svg [ Width "300"; Height "200" ] [
        gradientDef
        rect [ Fill "url(#grad1)"; Width "300"; Height "200" ] []
    ]

printfn "%s" (chart.ToString())
// Before fix: emits <radient ...> tags → invalid SVG, gradient silently ignored by browser
// After fix:  emits <linearGradient ...> tags → correct SVG
```

### Usage in practice

Neither `linearGradient` nor `radialGradient` are used anywhere in fsdocs itself (I checked) — they're purely for external library consumers building SVGs programmatically. That explains why the bug went undetected. Anyone using this API to render gradient-filled SVG charts would see a broken SVG (browsers silently drop unknown `<radient>` elements, so the fill would just disappear).

You're absolutely right, `@dsyme` and `@nojaf` — `fsdocs watch` only ever binds an HTTP server (no SSL certificates), so `window.location.protocol` will always be `"http:"` and the `wss:` branch was dead code.

I've pushed a simplification: [commit `956dd567`](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/commit/956dd567) removes the protocol-sniffing entirely and hardcodes `ws://`:

```javascript
var wsUri = "ws://" + window.location.host + "/websocket";
```

This is simpler and more honest about what the watch server actually supports.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Good catch, `@nojaf`! I've pushed a fix to this PR.

**Root cause**: when the tooltip is repositioned to stay within the viewport (flipped above the cursor or shifted left), there's a gap between the trigger and tooltip. The `mouseout` handler checked `relatedTarget` synchronously — any gap meant `relatedTarget` was empty space (not the tooltip), so `hideTip` fired before the mouse could cross it.

**Fix**: replaced the synchronous `hideTip` calls in both `mouseout` handlers with a 300 ms `scheduleHide`. The pending hide is cancelled immediately when the mouse enters the tooltip (`mouseover` on `div.fsdocs-tip`) or re-enters the trigger (top of `showTip`). This is the classic hover-intent pattern and works regardless of tooltip position — whether directly adjacent or repositioned across the viewport.

The `relatedTarget` guard checks are no longer needed (the delay handles all cases) and have been removed, simplifying the code.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I've implemented the nested navigation category feature in [a draft PR](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/pulls) (branch `repo-assist/improve-frontmatter-nestedcat-2026-03-21`, created by this run — closes #927).

**How to use it:**

Add a `/` separator to the `category` field in your front matter:

````yaml
---
category: Collections/Lists
categoryindex: 1
index: 2
---
```

This renders in the sidebar as:

```
Collections           ← parent header
  Arrays              ← sub-header
    - Pattern Matching
  Lists               ← sub-header
    - Destructuring with Cons
    - Pattern Matching
````

**Design choices:**
- The `/` separator was chosen to match Hugo/Docusaurus conventions and the approach suggested in `@1eyewonder`'s original proposal
- **Fully backwards compatible** — categories without `/` render identically to today
- One nesting level (parent/child) is supported; deeper nesting can be added later
- Parent category ordering is determined automatically by the minimum `categoryindex` of any child — no new front-matter key required
- When custom `_menu_template.html` files are used, the structure is flattened to the parent level so existing templates are unaffected

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Coming back to this thread with a clearer breakdown of the current behaviour and the recommended workflow.

## How images in XML doc comments work today

When you use `--mdcomments` and write:

````fsharp
/// ![local image](images/pi.png)
let pi = 3.14159
```

The markdown is parsed and rendered as `(img src="images/pi.png" alt="local image" /)` in the generated HTML. The image path is used verbatim — `fsdocs` does **not** copy source-tree files referenced inside XML doc comments.

This is by design: `fsdocs` has no way to know where `images/pi.png` is relative to the source file, and it doesn't walk the source tree looking for assets.

## Recommended workflow

Put images in the `docs/` input directory, which is copied to the output automatically:

```
your-project/
├── docs/
│   └── content/
│       └── images/
│           └── pi.png   ← put images here
└── src/
    └── MyLib/
        └── MyModule.fs
````

Then reference them in XML doc comments using a root-relative path:

```fsharp
/// (img src="/content/images/pi.png" alt="Pi diagram" /)
let pi = 3.14159
```

Or in markdown comment mode (`--mdcomments`):

```fsharp
/// ![Pi diagram](/content/images/pi.png)
let pi = 3.14159
```

The leading `/` gives a site-root-relative URL that works regardless of how deep the API page is nested in the output.

> **Note:** The `\{\{fsdocs-root}}` template substitution is applied to the *template file*, not to the content of XML doc comments. So `\{\{fsdocs-root}}content/images/pi.png` in a doc comment will not be substituted — use an absolute site path like `/content/images/pi.png` instead.

## For `.fsx` literate scripts (different story)

If your content is in `.fsx` or `.md` files under `docs/`, images in `(** ![...](path) *)` blocks **are** resolved relative to the source file and copied to the output when using `--saveimages=all`. That's a separate pipeline from API docs.

## What a proper fix would look like

Automatically copying source-tree images referenced in XML doc comments would require:
1. Knowing the source file location for each XML doc entry (available, it's in the cracked project)
2. Resolving image paths relative to the source file
3. Copying images to `output/content/images/` and rewriting the `src` attribute

This is a medium-complexity enhancement — if there's maintainer appetite for it, happy to attempt an implementation.

Checking in on this issue. The workaround `@nojaf` shared in January 2023 — passing the missing reference explicitly via `--fscoptions " -r:path/to/MyAssembly.dll"` — remains available in current versions.

A few notes that may help:

**Ensure projects are built before running `fsdocs build`**  
`fsdocs` does not build your projects — it cracks them (reads MSBuild metadata) and then generates docs from the pre-built output assemblies. If project A hasn't been compiled when fsdocs cracks project B (which depends on A), the cracking step can fail. Running `dotnet build` on the whole solution first generally resolves this.

**Build order from solution file**  
When running `fsdocs build` in a directory containing a `.sln` file, projects are processed in dependency order (project A before project B), which should help Ionide.ProjInfo resolve cross-project references correctly.

**Is this still reproducible in v22?**  
The original report was with v11.2.0. The project cracking code has been substantially reworked since then. If anyone can confirm whether this still occurs with the current version (`22.0.0-alpha.1`) — particularly with the demo repo at https://github.com/ArtemyB/FsDocsSample — that would help prioritise a proper fix. If `dotnet build` + `fsdocs build` now works correctly, it might be worth closing this issue.

🤖 *Repo Assist here.*

Great catch, `@nojaf`! There are actually three distinct `localhost` strings in play — let me clarify each:

**1. `userRoot` — the root URL baked into generated HTML links**
```fsharp
sprintf "(localhost/redacted) this.port_option
```
This is the one the `--root` option already overrides. The `localhost` default is fine for local development; users who need a different URL (Codespaces, proxy) pass `--root /` or `--root https://…`. ✅

**2. WebSocket hot-reload URL — injected into every page**
```javascript
var wsUri = "ws://localhost:\{\{PORT}}/websocket";
```
This was the real problem. In Codespaces or behind a proxy, the browser loads the page from a non-`localhost` origin and **cannot connect back to `ws://localhost:…`** — hot reload silently breaks. I've just pushed a fix: it now uses `window.location.host` (which includes the correct hostname + port) and picks `ws:` or `wss:` to match the page's protocol:
```javascript
var wsProtocol = window.location.protocol === "https:" ? "wss:" : "ws:";
var wsUri = wsProtocol + "//" + window.location.host + "/websocket";
```
This works correctly for local dev, Codespaces, and any proxied setup. ✅

**3. Server bind address**
The Suave server binds to `127.0.0.1` (Suave's default). In **GitHub Codespaces** this is fine — Codespaces tunnels traffic to `127.0.0.1` within the container. In **Docker with published ports** you'd need `0.0.0.0`, but that's a separate concern and a bigger security trade-off. I've left it as-is for now; we can add a `--host` option if that need is confirmed.

**Summary**: the `--root` option handles doc links; the WebSocket fix (just pushed) handles hot-reload; the server bind address is fine for the common cases.

I've investigated this end-to-end and submitted a draft PR with results. Here's what I found:

### End-to-end test setup

I created two helper assemblies that exactly mirror the issue description:

- **CrossAssemblyA**: `type Did = private | Did of string` (single-case private DU)
- **CrossAssemblyB**: `Subject` (DU with `Account of Did`) and `TeamMember` (record with `Did: Did`, `JoinedAt: DateTimeOffset option`)

I then wrote three tests that call `CodeFormatter.ParseAndCheckSource` with these assemblies and check that the hover tooltips show `Did` (not `obj`) and `DateTimeOffset` (not `obj`).

### Result

**All three tests pass** against FCS 43.10.100. The tooltip correctly renders:
```
type Subject =
  | Account of Did
  | Record of string
```
…and `TeamMember` shows `Did` and `DateTimeOffset` correctly. No `obj` substitution occurs.

### What this tells us

The current development version handles cross-assembly tooltips correctly. The `obj` you saw is likely caused by one of:

1. **`#r` path resolution failure** — when `GetProjectOptionsFromScript` can't find `AssemblyA.dll` (because the relative path doesn't match the script file's location), FCS still type-checks but with incomplete metadata. The *definition* tooltip (`type Subject = ...`) uses unresolvable types, which FCS erases to `obj`. The *value* tooltip (`val myDid: Did`) works because FCS can infer the type name from the check result rather than from assembly metadata traversal.

2. **FCS version** — `fsdocs-tool 21.0.0` uses an older FCS. This bug may have been fixed upstream in a later FCS release.

### Practical recommendation

To ensure tooltips are correct with `fsdocs`, use **absolute paths** in your `#r` directives:
```fsharp
#r @"/absolute/path/to/AssemblyA.dll"
#r @"/absolute/path/to/AssemblyB.dll"
```
Or place the `.fsx` script in the same directory as the referenced DLLs and use relative paths — my third test confirms this also works correctly.

The PR adds regression tests so this behaviour is protected going forward.

## Root cause

The failing test was `ConvertCommand omits fsdocs-tip divs when no template given` in `fsdocs-tool.Tests`. 

**All tests passed (87/87)**, but the `fsdocs-tool.Tests` suite had 1 failure that appeared earlier in the CI log. The test asserted:

```fsharp
html |> shouldNotContainText "fsdocs-tip"
```

This was originally written to verify that tooltip `(div class="fsdocs-tip")` elements are **not emitted** when no template is provided. However, since this PR changed `<span onmouseover=...>` to `<span data-fsdocs-tip="..." ...>`, the generated HTML now always contains `"fsdocs-tip"` as part of the data attribute names — causing the assertion to fail even though no tooltip divs are being emitted.

## Fix

Updated the assertion to be specific about what it's testing — the absence of tooltip *div* elements:

```fsharp
// Before
html |> shouldNotContainText "fsdocs-tip"

// After
html |> shouldNotContainText "class=\"fsdocs-tip\""
```

Committed and pushed. All 8 `fsdocs-tool.Tests` now pass.

Thanks for the detailed report — the pattern you've identified (same-assembly types render correctly, cross-assembly types show `obj`) is a useful signal.

### Where this likely originates

FSharp.Formatting uses the F# Compiler Service (FCS) `checkResults.GetToolTip(line, col, lineText, island, tokenTag)` API to generate hover tooltips for code snippets. The returned `ToolTipText` already contains fully-formatted text (as `TaggedText[]`); FSharp.Formatting just converts that to HTML without any type-name transformation of its own.

This means the `obj` substitution is happening **inside FCS**, specifically in its `GetToolTip` → `MainDescription` formatting path. The relevant FSharp.Formatting code is in [`CodeFormatAgent.fs:245`](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/blob/main/src/FSharp.Formatting.CodeFormat/CodeFormatAgent.fs#L245) and [`ToolTipReader.fs:65`](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/blob/main/src/FSharp.Formatting.CodeFormat/ToolTipReader.fs#L65) — there's nothing there that would replace type names.

### Most likely FCS root cause

When FCS formats a type for a tooltip, it traverses `FSharpType.TypeDefinition`. If the assembly containing that type definition cannot be resolved during formatting (e.g. because the `FSharpCheckFileResults` was produced with different `OtherOptions` than those that are later used for type resolution), FCS may fall back to the erased representation — which for many types is `obj`.

A concrete trigger: `GetProjectOptionsFromScript` resolves `#r "AssemblyA.dll"` relative to the **script file path** passed to it. If the script file is being processed from a different working directory (e.g. a temp directory or a project output path), the relative `#r` paths may resolve incorrectly — the project options end up with wrong assembly paths, the check succeeds (FCS finds the DLLs for compilation), but when `GetToolTip` later formats cross-assembly types the symbols are in an unresolvable state.

### Things to try

1. **Use absolute paths in `#r`**: replace `#r "AssemblyA.dll"` with `#r "/absolute/path/to/AssemblyA.dll"`. If this fixes the tooltips, it confirms the relative-path theory above.
2. **Check if both DLLs are in the same directory** as the `.fsx` script when `fsdocs build` is run.
3. **Run with `--verbose`** to see what assembly paths end up in the FCS project options.

### Upstream tracking

This is very likely an FCS issue. If absolute paths don't help, it would be worth filing a report at [dotnet/fsharp](https://github.com/dotnet/fsharp/issues) with a minimal FCS reproduction (calling `GetToolTip` directly on a checked file that `#r`s a cross-assembly DLL). FSharp.Formatting would then pick up the fix automatically once FCS is updated.

Picking up on `@nhirschey`'s comment — the "websocket errors and shutdown messages" you described are a symptom. Here's a diagnosis based on the current source:

**What `fsdocs watch` does**

When running correctly, it:
1. Does an initial build of all docs
2. Starts a local Suave HTTP server (port 8901 by default) with a WebSocket endpoint
3. Injects a hot-reload `(script)` tag into every generated page so the browser reconnects and refreshes when files change
4. Watches `docs/`, `_template.html`, and output DLLs for changes; triggers a rebuild on any change
5. Holds until you press `Ctrl+C`

**Most likely causes for the "exits immediately" behaviour**

1. **Missing `index.md`**: the docs say the input directory must contain at least `index.md`. Without it, the initial build produces no pages, the server starts but the browser reports a 404, and the websocket is never injected — so the browser shows a connection error before fsdocs has a chance to print the "Press Ctrl+C" prompt.

2. **Running inside F# Interactive**: The `>` prompt you mention suggests fsdocs was invoked from FSI (`#r "fsdocs-tool.dll"` style) or from a task runner that embeds FSI. In that context, the `Console.CancelKeyPress` handler that keeps watch alive may not behave the same as in a standalone terminal process. `fsdocs watch` is designed to be run directly from a shell, not hosted inside FSI.

3. **Build error on startup**: If the initial build fails (e.g. the project can't be cracked, or a script has type errors), fsdocs may exit before reaching the `waitForKey` loop.

**Quick diagnostic**

From a plain terminal (not inside any REPL):

```bash
mkdir -p demo/docs
echo "# Hello" > demo/docs/index.md
cd demo
dotnet fsdocs watch --noapidocs
```

If that works, the issue is in your project setup, not fsdocs itself.

Since this was reported in March 2024, things may have improved in the current release. If you're still hitting this on the latest `fsdocs` version, sharing the exact console output (including any `ERROR` lines from the startup) would help narrow it down further.

🤖 *Repo Assist here.*

**No — the "Verify" pipeline does not run during CI.**

Here's how the pipelines are wired:

| Pipeline | `runIfOnlySpecified` | When it runs |
|---|---|---|
| `CI` | `false` | Always — default when no `-p` flag is given |
| `Verify` | `true` | Only when explicitly called: `dotnet fsi build.fsx -- -p Verify` |

The CI workflow (`pull-requests.yml`) runs `dotnet fsi build.fsx` with no arguments, so it always executes the `CI` pipeline and **never** the `Verify` pipeline.

The `CheckDocScripts` stage I added to `Verify` is purely a **local developer convenience** — a quick pre-push check that catches doc-script type errors without running the full CI pipeline (which would need to pack NuGet artifacts first). The actual doc-script type-checking in CI is already handled by the `GenerateDocs` stage in the `CI` pipeline, which installs fsdocs from the packed NuGet artifacts and runs `fsdocs build --strict`.

So the value of adding `CheckDocScripts` to `Verify` is marginal (only helps developers who already run `dotnet fsi build.fsx -- -p Verify` locally after a `dotnet build`). If you'd prefer to drop that addition and keep `Verify` unchanged, that's entirely reasonable — I'm happy to revert just that part.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Thanks for filing this! I've investigated the issue and created a PR with the following fixes:

**Root cause:** `docs/evaluation.fsx` called `FsiEvaluator()` without the `new` keyword in two places, generating FS0760 IDisposable warnings on every docs build (printed 5× due to multi-format rendering). Separately, the `ParseScript` error message when type errors were found didn't include the file path, making diagnosis harder.

**What the PR does:**

1. **Fixes `docs/evaluation.fsx`** — changes both `FsiEvaluator()` → `new FsiEvaluator()` to eliminate the FS0760 warnings.

2. **Improves the error message** in `ParseScript` to include the file path when type errors are found.

3. **Adds `CheckDocScripts` to the `Verify` pipeline** in `build.fsx` — so `dotnet fsi build.fsx -- -p Verify` now also runs `fsdocs build --strict`, catching doc-script type errors locally. The full CI pipeline already had this check (via `GenerateDocs`), but it wasn't reachable from the lightweight `Verify` pipeline that developers typically use pre-push.

Note: the original MarkdownRange type errors mentioned in the issue are not present in the current source (that API change was already landed — `range` fields are now `MarkdownRange`, not `MarkdownRange option`). What remained were the FS0760 warnings from the IDisposable usage.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I've pushed a new commit addressing both questions and adding the manual validation directory.

---

### Q1: No default template without `--template`?

**By design.** When `--template` is omitted, `fsdocs convert` produces a raw HTML fragment (document body only — no `(html)` wrapper). This matches the pre-PR behaviour and is intentional.

Running `dotnet fsdocs.dll convert docs/content.fsx` is identical to `fsdocs convert docs/content.fsx` — the `.dll` vs no-extension doesn't affect anything. You need to pass `--template fsdocs` explicitly to get the built-in site template.

### Q2: Substitutions not being applied?

**Yes, this was a real bug — now fixed.** The fsdocs default template uses ~20 `\{\{fsdocs-*}}` parameters (page title, collection name, nav links, etc.) that `fsdocs build` populates from the project. The `fsdocs convert` command wasn't supplying any of these, so they stayed as literal `\{\{fsdocs-page-title}}` text in the output.

**Fix**: `fsdocs convert` now supplies sensible defaults for all standard template parameters when a template is used:
- `\{\{fsdocs-page-title}}` → input filename (without extension)
- `\{\{fsdocs-collection-name}}` → input filename (without extension)
- `\{\{fsdocs-source-basename}}` → input filename (without extension)
- `\{\{fsdocs-body-class}}` → `content`
- Nav links (`\{\{fsdocs-license-link}}` etc.) → `#`
- All others → `""` (empty string)

User-supplied `--parameters` values always override these defaults. No `\{\{fsdocs-*}}` placeholders remain in the output.

Smoke-tested: `fsdocs convert example.md --template fsdocs` now produces a clean `(title)example | example(/title)` with no raw placeholders.

---

### New: `tests/manual/convert/`

Added `tests/manual/convert/` with:
- **`README.md`** — step-by-step validation walkthrough for 7 scenarios (raw, minimal template, built-in template, custom params, F# script, no-embed, LaTeX)
- **`example.md`** — markdown test input with headings, code block, table, links
- **`example.fsx`** — F# literate script test input  
- **`_template-minimal.html`** — a simple template using only `\{\{fsdocs-content}}`, `\{\{fsdocs-page-title}}`, and the CSS links — recommended for `fsdocs convert` use where project-level nav data isn't needed

Even though author rules should win, the `canvas` keyword can resolve unexpectedly in Chrome's top layer when `color-scheme` is involved. I've created a draft PR implementing a View Transitions experiment for the default fsdocs template. Here's a summary of what was added:

**Cross-document navigation transitions** (`fsdocs-default.css`)

```css
`@view-transition` {
    navigation: auto;
}
```

This single CSS at-rule opts every generated docs page into the browser's cross-document View Transitions machinery. Users on Chrome 126+ and Safari 18.2+ will see a smooth 200ms fade when navigating between doc pages — no JavaScript needed. Browsers without support silently ignore the rule.

Named layout regions (`header`, `#fsdocs-main-menu`, `main`) are given `view-transition-name` values so each animates independently.

**Theme toggle animation** (`fsdocs-theme-toggle.js`)

The light/dark switch is now wrapped in `document.startViewTransition()` (with a synchronous fallback) for a smooth animated cross-fade instead of an instant flip.

The changes are entirely progressive — Firefox users and any other browser without support see zero difference.

> Generated by [Repo Assist](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/actions/runs/22845418606)

🤖 *Repo Assist here — pushed an update addressing your feedback.*

## Changes in this update

### 1. Black-background regression — fixed

**Root cause:** The browser UA stylesheet for `[popover]` applies `inset: 0` to `:popover-open` elements, which is shorthand for `top: 0; right: 0; bottom: 0; left: 0`. Our JavaScript only overrides `left` and `top` via inline styles, leaving `right: 0` and `bottom: 0` in effect. Combined with our `margin: 0` override (which removed the UA's `margin: auto` that had contained the element to `width/height: fit-content`), the element stretched across the rest of the viewport — visible as a dark area behind the tooltip.

**Fix:** Added `inset: unset` and an explicit `position: fixed` to `div.fsdocs-tip:popover-open` in the CSS, so the UA `inset: 0` is fully reset and the JS-supplied `left`/`top` coordinates are the only positioning in effect.

### 2. Dropped the fallback

Removed the `else` branch in `fsdocs-tips.js` that fell back to `display: block` / `position: absolute` for older browsers. The Popover API is Baseline 2024 (Chrome 114+, Firefox 125+, Safari 17+) — agreed that the fsdocs technical audience will be well covered.

### 3. Subtle fade-in animation

Added a 120ms ease-out opacity fade-in (`@keyframes fsdocs-tip-fade-in`) applied on `:popover-open`. Quick and unobtrusive.

The `document.onkeydown = hideUsingEsc` handler was also removed — the Popover API's built-in Escape-key dismissal for `auto` popovers handles that natively.

Done! A prototype PR is on its way — it implements the self-contained HTML output as discussed.

**What the PR does:**

- **`--embed-resources` is on by default** for HTML output when a template is used — all local CSS, JS, and images are inlined into the output file so it renders correctly when opened directly in a browser or shared as an attachment.
- **`--template fsdocs`** new sentinel value: reads the built-in default template from the embedded assembly resource, so you don't need a local `_template.html`.
- **`--no-embed-resources`** opt-out flag to keep external references (original behaviour).

**Usage:**

```bash
# Self-contained file with built-in styling — no companion files needed
fsdocs convert my-script.fsx --template fsdocs

# Custom template with auto-embedding (default)
fsdocs convert my-script.fsx --template _template.html

# Opt out
fsdocs convert my-script.fsx --template _template.html --no-embed-resources
```

The implementation is a regex-based HTML post-processor that runs after `Literate.ConvertXxxFile` writes the output. Remote URLs (Google Fonts, CDN icons, etc.) are left as external links — only local file paths are inlined. All 499 existing tests pass.

A couple of known rough edges for this prototype: the regex approach is simple and doesn't handle all edge cases (an HTML parser would be more robust); and remote resources (fonts, CDN icons) aren't fetched. Happy to iterate based on feedback.

`@dsyme` Reasonable case for yes — for `fsdocs convert` specifically the most natural mental model is "give me a single self-contained file I can send/share/embed", so breaking output that silently references missing CSS/JS would be more surprising than a slightly larger self-contained file.

A few considerations for making it the default:

**For defaulting to embedded:**
- `fsdocs convert` doesn't copy a `content/` directory alongside the output, so the default non-embedded HTML would typically be broken unless the user manually serves it from the right directory
- The "share one file" use case is the whole point of `convert` vs `build`
- Pandoc made the same choice for their `--embed-resources` equivalent

**For keeping it opt-in:**
- Power users building a collection of converted files might want to share one `content/` folder across all outputs
- Larger files by default

My suggestion: default to embedding for `fsdocs convert` (opposite of `fsdocs build`), and provide `--no-embed-resources` as the opt-out. The "broken by default" outcome for the non-embedding case is a stronger argument than the file-size concern.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Thanks for the question, and thanks `@nhirschey` for the existing context. Here's my analysis:

---

## Why `HtmlModel` is internal (and probably should stay that way)

`HtmlModel.fs` is a rendering implementation detail — it's the *output sink* used by `fsdocs` to write HTML without string concatenation. It was never designed as a public AST for downstream consumers. Making it public would create a surface-area commitment (versioning, docs, compatibility) for an internal plumbing type that doesn't add much beyond what you could do with `string` or your own library.

## What you actually want: the `MarkdownDocument` AST

The public `MarkdownDocument` type with its `MarkdownParagraph` and `MarkdownSpan` DUs **is** the semantic AST you want to work with. It's rich, fully public, and models the document structure semantically (headings, paragraphs, lists, links, images, code blocks, tables, etc.).

**The practical options:**

**Option 1 — Embed as raw HTML string (simplest)**
```fsharp
let markdownHtml = Markdown.ToHtml(content)  // returns a string

let page =
    html [] [
        div [] [ str "This is my header" ]
        rawText markdownHtml   // most HTML DSLs have a raw/unsafe HTML embed
        div [] [ str "This is my footer" ]
    ]
```
If your HTML library supports inserting raw HTML (Feliz has `Html.custom`, Giraffe.ViewEngine has `rawText`, etc.), this is the most direct solution.

**Option 2 — Walk `MarkdownDocument` and map to your HTML DSL**
```fsharp
let doc = Markdown.Parse(content)

let rec spanToNode = function
    | Literal(text, _) -> str text
    | Strong(spans, _) -> b [] (List.map spanToNode spans)
    | Emphasis(spans, _) -> em [] (List.map spanToNode spans)
    | DirectLink(spans, url, title, _) -> a [ href url ] (List.map spanToNode spans)
    // ... etc.

let rec paraToNode = function
    | Heading(size, spans, _) -> h size [] (List.map spanToNode spans)
    | Paragraph(spans, _) -> p [] (List.map spanToNode spans)
    | ListBlock(Unordered, items, _) -> ul [] (items |> List.map (fun item -> li [] (List.collect (List.map paraToNode) item)))
    // ... etc.

let markdownNodes = doc.Paragraphs |> List.map paraToNode
```
This gives you a first-class HTML AST in your own library's types. It's more work but gives you full control over rendering, CSS classes, etc.

## Should `HtmlModel` be made public?

Probably not as-is — it's tightly coupled to `fsdocs`'s internal needs (e.g. `UniqueNameGenerator`, `FormattingContext`) and not general-purpose. The `MarkdownDocument` AST is the right abstraction boundary. If there's a specific transformation you need that the current `Markdown.ToHtml` doesn't support (custom CSS classes, heading IDs, etc.), that would be a better enhancement request.

**My recommendation:** Option 1 (raw HTML embed) unless you need fine-grained control over the HTML structure, in which case Option 2 is the path forward.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Happy to chime in! Having read the thread, here's my take:

**The `(*** condition:prepare ***)` approach is the right long-term pattern.** It's what FSharp.Data, Fantomas, Plotly.NET, and DiffSharp all converged on, and for good reason:

- No NuGet caching headaches — you're loading the DLL you just built
- Docs can be built at any point during development, not just after a publish
- End-users see the clean `#r "nuget:"` snippet in published docs because the prepare block is hidden
- External dependencies can still use `#r "nuget:"` normally (they're already on NuGet)

The rough pattern is:

```fsharp
(*** condition:prepare ***)
#r "../path/to/output/YourLibrary.dll"
#r "nuget: SomeDependency"

(*** hide ***)
// This is what readers see in the rendered docs
```

```markdown
To use this library, add:
    #r "nuget: YourLibrary, {{fsdocs-package-version}}"
```

**The local nuget feed approach** (`nuget.config` pointing at a local packages dir) is workable too, but it has the cache invalidation problem you hit, and it requires a pack step before docs. I'd treat it as a fallback.

**One thing worth noting as a potential fsdocs improvement**: it would be nice if `fsdocs` supported a `--packages-dir` flag or a nuget.config substitution that let you override package sources at doc-generation time without touching the scripts themselves. That way you could write `#r "nuget: YourLibrary"` in your scripts and have it redirect to a local feed only during doc builds. If this is a pattern people want, it might be worth an enhancement issue — the `(*** condition:prepare ***)` workaround is effective but a bit awkward to set up for the first time.

For now I'd recommend following the FSharp.Data/Fantomas pattern — it's battle-tested.

Great feature request! The `--embed-resources` idea maps cleanly onto the pandoc model and would make `fsdocs convert` genuinely useful for sharing standalone documents or embedding them into other workflows.

**Dependency:** this feature sits on top of PR #1019 (the `fsdocs convert` command itself), so it will need to follow once that PR lands.

**Implementation sketch:**

Once `fsdocs convert` has produced the HTML from the processed script, a post-processing pass with `--embed-resources` would:

1. **Parse the output HTML** – scan for `(link rel="stylesheet" href="...")` and `(script src="...")` tags that point to local files.
2. **Inline CSS** – replace each `(link rel="stylesheet" href="path/to/file.css")` with `(style)/* contents of file.css */(/style)`.
3. **Inline JS** – replace each `(script src="path/to/file.js")(/script)` with `(script)/* contents of file.js */(/script)`.
4. **Inline local images** – replace `(img src="path/to/img.png")` (local paths only) with `(img src="(redacted)

The path resolution should be relative to the template directory (or the generated file's location), matching the same base that `fsdocs build` uses when copying the `content/` directory.

**Notes:**
- Remote URLs (`(redacted)`) can be left as-is or optionally fetched — I'd suggest leaving them as-is by default, or adding a separate `--embed-remote` flag later.
- The default fsdocs template references several CSS/JS files from `content/` (loaded relative to the output), so the path resolution logic needs to be consistent with how the template references those files.
- The resulting file will be significantly larger but truly self-contained — a good trade-off for the "share a single file" use case.

The proposed CLI design (`--template fsdocs` / `--template _template.html` + `--embed-resources`) looks sensible. If there's a preference to default to the built-in template when no `_template.html` exists in the project, a `--template fsdocs` flag as a special sentinel value makes that discoverable.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Great discussion! This relates to the recently opened issue #1064 ("Consider different docs for different TFMs"), which focuses specifically on the multi-TFM/multi-target API documentation problem.

Here's how the two topics connect and some thoughts on feasibility:

**What's currently possible**

The `(*** condition: fsx ***)` / `(*** condition: prepare ***)` system in literate scripts lets you write target-conditional *code examples* today. For selecting between Fable vs. CLR *API documentation*, there's no built-in mechanism yet.

**The core challenge**

fsdocs generates API docs from compiled assemblies (DLLs) via FSharp Compiler Services. A multi-target project produces multiple outputs (e.g. `net8.0/MyLib.dll`, `netstandard2.0/MyLib.dll`). At the moment, fsdocs picks one TFM and generates a single set of docs — it doesn't merge or diff the outputs.

For the Fable/WebSharper/F#-to-JS case specifically, those compilers typically don't produce a standard .NET assembly at all, so FCS-based API introspection wouldn't apply directly. An extension point would need a different discovery mechanism.

**Feasible near-term steps**

1. **TFM picker** (like MSDN's `.NET 8` / `.NET Framework` dropdown): generate separate API doc sets per TFM and add a switcher UI. See #1064 for a more detailed breakdown.
2. **Conditional doc markup**: allow XML doc comments to annotate availability, e.g. `/// (platform name="Fable"/)`, which fsdocs would render as an availability badge — requiring no assembly-level change.
3. **devdocs.io integration**: fsdocs already generates structured HTML; the main work would be producing the `entries.json` / `index.json` manifest that devdocs.io expects. This would be a useful standalone contribution independent of the multi-target discussion.

The "filtered view" idea you describe is essentially what the TFM picker in #1064 would provide at the .NET level. Extending it to Fable/WebSharper targets would require either a custom plugin mechanism or separate doc-generation tooling (since those ecosystems don't produce standard .NET symbols).

Happy to expand on any of these approaches if it would help move things forward!

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Great observation from `@TheAngryByrd`! This is a significant architectural consideration. Here's a brief analysis:

## Current Situation

`fsdocs` currently runs project cracking once and generates a single set of API docs. When a project targets multiple TFMs (e.g., `net6.0;net8.0`), Ionide.ProjInfo returns separate `ProjectOptions` per TFM — but only one set of symbol data ends up in the docs.

## Implementation Options

### Option A: TFM selector UI (like MSDN / .NET API Browser)
- Generate API docs for each TFM separately, then merge into a single HTML page with a dropdown to toggle between views
- Complex to implement; requires significant changes to the HTML template and CSS
- Most user-friendly for consumers of the docs

### Option B: Lowest-common-denominator
- Only document APIs that exist in **all** targeted TFMs (intersection)
- Much simpler; avoids a UI change
- Loses visibility of TFM-conditional APIs (e.g., APIs guarded by `#if NET8_0_OR_GREATER`)

### Option C: Per-TFM output directories
- Generate a separate docs subfolder per TFM, let users navigate between them
- Avoids merging logic; easy to implement
- Less seamless UX than a selector

### Option D: Single "richest" TFM
- Pick the TFM with the most APIs (typically the latest) and document that
- Simple, but hides APIs only available on older TFMs

## Suggested Approach

Option A is the gold standard, but Options B or D could be a pragmatic interim approach with a clear migration path toward A later. The key challenge in any option is the `ProjectCracker` phase — it needs to surface multi-TFM awareness so `GenerateModel` can decide how to aggregate.

This is a moderately large feature. A good first step would be to add structured tracking of which TFM each `CrackedProject` was built for, so the information is available when generating models.

Done! I've pushed a commit that adds tests and documentation. Here's a summary of what was added — and a bug fix I found along the way.

---

## Example

The `(*** include-toc ***)` command must be placed as a **standalone command** between `(**...*) ` blocks (not inside one, since `***)` would close the preceding `(**`):

```fsharp
(**
# My Document
*)
(*** include-toc:2 ***)
(**
## Section One

Some content here.

## Section Two

More content here.
*)
```

This renders an unordered list of links at the position of `(*** include-toc:2 ***)`:
- [My Document](#My-Document)
- [Section One](#Section-One)
- [Section Two](#Section-Two)

Use `(*** include-toc ***)` for the default depth of 3, or `(*** include-toc:N ***)` to limit to headings up to depth N.

---

## What was added

**5 new tests** in `LiterateTests.fs`:
1. `include-toc parses as TableOfContents with default depth 3`
2. `include-toc:2 parses as TableOfContents with depth 2`
3. `include-toc generates anchor links for all headings in HTML output`
4. `include-toc:N filters out headings deeper than N`
5. `include-toc handles duplicate heading names with unique anchors`

All 121 tests pass.

**Documentation** added to `docs/literate.fsx` — a new "Table of Contents" section with a command table and example.

---

## Bug fix

I found that `generateTableOfContents` was only called in the `transformDocument` path (`Formatting.fs`) but **not** in the direct `Literate.ToHtml`, `WriteHtml`, `ToLatex`, `WriteLatex`, `ToPynb`, and `ToFsx` methods. This meant the `(*** include-toc ***)` command would be silently dropped when using those APIs directly. I've fixed all six call sites in `Literate.fs`.

Following up on `@dsyme`'s confirmation of interest and `@nojaf`'s mention of the Popover API — I've opened a draft PR implementing the Popover API approach:

**PR: [Use Popover API for code snippet tooltips; fix scroll-offset positioning](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/pull/1060)**

### What it does

- Adds `popover` attribute to every `div.fsdocs-tip` element generated by `HtmlFormatting.fs`, placing them in the browser [top layer]((developer.mozilla.org/redacted) on modern browsers.
- Updates `fsdocs-tips.js` to call `showPopover()` / `hidePopover()` when available (graceful fallback to `display: block/none` for older browsers).
- Switches to `position: fixed` in the Popover API path — this is correct for top-layer elements and also **fixes the existing scroll-offset bug** where tooltips appeared offset from the cursor when the page was scrolled.
- All existing tests pass; no API changes.

### Note on the Popover API vs. full CSS-only

The Popover API still requires a small amount of JS to:
1. Position the popover near the cursor on `mouseover`, and
2. Call `showPopover()` / `hidePopover()`.

A fully CSS-only tooltip (zero JS) would require restructuring the generated HTML so each tooltip `div` is a sibling of its trigger `span`, enabling CSS `:hover` + adjacent sibling rules. That's a larger change. The Popover API approach gives most of the benefits (top-layer rendering, proper Escape dismiss, accessibility) with minimal code changes.

Reviving this thread with an implementation analysis, since this issue has had no updates since 2020.

**What's being requested**

Group overloaded methods (e.g. multiple `Expr.Call` signatures) into a single entry in the member list, with their individual overloads accessible either inline or via a dedicated sub-page.

**Current behaviour**

Each overload is listed as a separate member entry with its full signature. For heavily-overloaded types (e.g. `Array.map`, `String.Format`, or F# quotation expressions), this creates a lot of repetition and makes the page hard to scan.

**Where the relevant code lives**

- `src/FSharp.Formatting.ApiDocs/GenerateModel.fs` — `ApiDocMember` list is built per FCS symbol; grouping would happen here, probably by member `CompiledName`/`DisplayName`.
- `src/FSharp.Formatting.ApiDocs/GenerateHtml.fs` — renders the member table/list; would need a grouped rendering mode.

**Implementation sketch**

1. In `GenerateModel.fs` (or a new pass), group `ApiDocMember` entries that share the same name and kind (method vs. property etc.) into an `ApiDocMemberGroup`.
2. In the HTML generator, render each group as a single row/section with an expandable overload list (similar to how docs.microsoft.com collapses overloads under one heading).
3. A dedicated overload page could be generated per group (e.g. `reference/mytype.html#Call` with anchor), matching the fsharp-core-docs style you linked.

**Complexity**

This is a non-trivial feature — it touches the core model, HTML generation, and potentially URL generation. It's also a potentially breaking change for anyone deep-linking to individual overload anchors. An opt-in MSBuild flag (e.g. `(FsDocsGroupOverloads)true(/FsDocsGroupOverloads)`) would be the safest approach.

Happy to attempt a PR for this if there is maintainer interest — it would be a good follow-on to the recent API-docs improvements (#590, #591).

The fix is minimal and surgical — only `ToolTipReader.fs` and `RELEASE_NOTES.md` are changed. All 20 `FSharp.CodeFormat.Tests` pass.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Great analysis by `@nojaf` in the earlier comments — the root cause is that the F# compiler's `NicePrint.fs` started including parameter attributes like `[(Optional)]` and `[(DefaultParameterValue)]` in method signatures passed to tooltip clients.

There are two paths forward:

### Path 1: Fix in the F# compiler (upstream)
As nojaf described, add a flag to `DisplayEnvironment` in the compiler to suppress parameter attributes in tooltip output. This is the cleanest fix but requires a PR to `dotnet/fsharp`.

### Path 2: Filter attributes in fsdocs (fsdocs-side workaround)
For the **code-snippet tooltips** (not API docs), the tooltip text flows through `src/FSharp.Formatting.CodeFormat/ToolTipReader.fs`. Specifically:
- `formatElement` processes `ToolTipElement.Group(items)`, reading `it.MainDescription` — a `TaggedText array` from FCS
- `linesFromTaggedText` converts that to string lines

A practical filter could be applied just before rendering — stripping attribute annotations from each line with a regex like `\[<[^\]]*>\]` (repeated to handle multiple attributes). For example, the function `linesFromTaggedText` could filter out tags with `TextTag.Attribute` or a post-processing step could strip `[<...>]` blocks from the resulting lines.

Here's a rough sketch of the post-processing approach in `ToolTipReader.fs`:

```fsharp
open System.Text.RegularExpressions

// Strip [(Attribute)] and [(Attribute(args))] from tooltip signature lines
let private stripParameterAttributes (line: string) =
    // Remove [<...>] attribute annotations (possibly multiple), plus surrounding whitespace
    Regex.Replace(line, @"\s*\[<[^\]]*>\]", "")
```

This would be called on each line produced by `linesFromTaggedText` in `formatElement`. It's a conservative filter — only removing content that matches the `[<...>]` pattern.

Note: This affects **code-snippet hover tooltips** in literate documents/notebooks. The API reference documentation has its own rendering pipeline and would need a separate fix.

Would the maintainers like a PR implementing the fsdocs-side filter? I'm happy to prepare one. Alternatively, the upstream compiler fix described by nojaf is also achievable if someone wants to take that route.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Great question, `@nojaf` — and not controversial at all! It's worth examining honestly. Here's the picture as best I can reconstruct it:

---

## Why we have our own parser

**Historical reason**: The parser was written by Tomas Petricek in 2012 (the copyright header says so). Back then, .NET markdown libraries were sparse or immature. The parser was originally part of [FSharp.Formatting](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting) as a standalone component that other F# tooling could use.

**Genuine technical reasons that still apply**:

1. **F#-idiomatic discriminated union AST** — The `MarkdownParagraph` / `MarkdownSpan` DU is deeply pattern-matched throughout the codebase (`Transformations.fs`, `Formatting.fs`, `HtmlFormatting.fs`, etc.). External parsers like [Markdig](https://github.com/xoofx/markdig) use a mutable OO visitor model. Wrapping that in a DU would be non-trivial.

2. **Source range tracking** — Every AST node carries a `MarkdownRange option` (line/column). This is how the literate programming pipeline knows where code snippets live in the source. Not all libraries expose this, and it needs to be correct.

3. **`EmbedParagraphs` / `EmbedSpans` extensibility** — The literate layer injects custom `LiterateParagraph` nodes (e.g. `CodeReference`, `FsiOutputReference`, `ItValueReference`) into the markdown AST by implementing `MarkdownEmbedParagraphs`. This tight extensibility is unusual for off-the-shelf parsers.

4. **The `(*** ... ***)` command syntax** — The `.fsx` literate script commands are entirely custom; no off-the-shelf parser handles them.

5. **Jupyter notebook support** — `OutputBlock`, `executionCount` fields in `CodeBlock`, `PynbFormatting.fs` — these require an AST shape that matches the notebook cell model.

6. **LaTeX/math** — `LatexBlock`, `LatexInlineMath`, `LatexDisplayMath` are first-class AST nodes. Markdig has a math extension but it's a plugin, not structural.

---

## Is migration realistic?

The most viable candidate would be **[Markdig](https://github.com/xoofx/markdig)** — it's CommonMark-compliant, actively maintained, and used by Microsoft Docs. However:

- **Full migration** would require converting `MarkdownDocument` consumers to a new AST — a very large breaking change across the whole codebase.
- **Hybrid approach**: use Markdig as the *parse engine* but translate its output into our existing `MarkdownParagraph` DU. This preserves the public API and all downstream code. The tricky parts would be (a) the custom literate commands, (b) range info, and (c) the `EmbedParagraphs` injection points.

---

## My take

The custom parser is a real maintenance burden (as issue #191 shows — chasing CommonMark edge cases one by one). But the escape hatch of "just replace it with Markdig" is less simple than it looks, because the codebase is built around the F# DU AST, not just the parsing step.

The **lowest-risk path forward** might be:
- Keep our parser for now
- Continue incremental CommonMark compliance improvements (like the recent ATX heading fix in #1028 and the setext/thematic break PR #1046)
- Track compliance gaps using the existing `CommonMarkSpecTest.fs` test suite

If there's appetite to invest more significantly, the hybrid Markdig translation approach could be explored in a separate issue — it would get us CommonMark compliance "for free" at the cost of some translation work.

Happy to investigate the Markdig translation approach in more depth if that's of interest!

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I've investigated the CI failure closely. Here's what happened and what I've fixed:

## Root Cause

The CI failure (`System.ArgumentException: The input list was empty`) was caused by a **parser quirk in `Ionide.KeepAChangelog 0.1.8`**: a blank line between items within a subsection (e.g. `### Added`) causes the library to **stop parsing the entire changelog**, resulting in `result.Releases` being an empty list.

The blank line was introduced by the merge commit `6cc9166f` (merging `main` into the PR), which combined the PR's RELEASE_NOTES additions with main's items and accidentally left a blank line separator between them:

````
* `fsdocs convert` now accepts `-o` as a shorthand for `--output`.
                                              <-- this blank line broke parsing
* Add `///` documentation comments to all public types...
```

I confirmed this by testing `Ionide.KeepAChangelog 0.1.8` directly:
- **With blank line** → `Releases count = 0` → `List.head` throws  
- **Without blank line** → `Releases count = 1` → works fine

The blank line was already removed by commit `0420bd48` (your local fix), so CI is now green.

## What I've Added

I've pushed a follow-up commit that replaces `result.Releases |> List.head` with an explicit pattern match that gives a **clear, actionable error message** if this happens again:

```
RELEASE_NOTES.md has no versioned releases.
Note: blank lines between items inside a section (e.g. '### Added')
cause Ionide.KeepAChangelog 0.1.8 to stop parsing — remove them.
````

This prevents the cryptic `The input list was empty` error in future.

I've implemented the tolerant cross-reference resolution as described above and created a draft PR for review.

**What was implemented:**

The root cause was two issues in `CrossReferenceResolver.fs`:

1. **`SymbolReader.fs` line 652** prepends `"T:"` to any cref without a colon, so `(see cref="Class2.Other" /)` becomes `"T:Class2.Other"`. The type lookup fails (no type named `"Class2.Other"`), and the code fell through to a spurious `.NET` docs link.

2. **`resolveCrossReferenceForTypeByXmlSig`** had no fallback for the `Type.Member` case after the `T:` prepend.

**Fix:**
- When a `T:Type.Member` sig fails both `xmlDocNameToSymbol` and `niceNameEntityLookup` lookups, split on the last `.` and try `niceNameEntityLookup` with the type part (handling both `"Class2"` and `"GenericClass2`1"` via raw + stripped lookup)
- Added `tryResolveUnqualifiedCref` for the rare case where the cref truly has no prefix at all
- Improved `tryResolveCrossReferenceForMemberByXmlSig` fallback similarly

**Supported patterns now (all resolving to internal links when the type is in the documented assembly set):**
- `(see cref="MyType" /)` → type page
- `(see cref="MyType.MyMember" /)` → member anchor
- `(see cref="GenericClass2`1.Property" /)` → member anchor on generic type
- `(see cref="GenericClass2`1" /)` → already worked; still works

All 337 tests pass. A draft PR has been created on branch `repo-assist/fix-issue-605-tolerant-crefs`.

**This feature appears to already be implemented.** Here's what I found:

The `CrossReferenceResolver.fs` in `src/FSharp.Formatting.ApiDocs/` contains an `externalDocsLink` function (around line 256) that specifically handles FSharp.Core types:

```fsharp
let externalDocsLink isMember simple (typeName: string) (fullName: string) =
    if
        fullName.StartsWith("FSharp.", StringComparison.Ordinal)
        || fullName.StartsWith("Microsoft.FSharp.", StringComparison.Ordinal)
    then
        let docs =
            noParen
                .Replace("Microsoft.FSharp.", "FSharp.")
                .Replace("``", "-")
                .Replace("`", "-")
                .Replace(".", "-")
                .ToLower()
        let link = sprintf "(fsharp.github.io/redacted) docs
        ...
    else
        // .NET types → learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/...
```

For `float` specifically, this would generate `(fsharp.github.io/redacted), since `float` is `Microsoft.FSharp.Core.float` in FCS. The function also includes a display-name mapping for types like `FSharpList` → `list`, `FSharpOption` → `option`, etc.

This code path is triggered whenever a type is not in the local assembly being documented (i.e., external types). For a library that uses `float`, `int`, `list`, etc. as parameter/return types, the generated HTML should contain hyperlinks to the corresponding FSharp.Core docs page.

The feature was also mentioned in the release notes: *"generate HTML giving hyperlinks for types with cross-links"* (though without a specific issue number tied to this one).

If you're seeing `float` without links in recently generated docs, it may be worth checking whether the fsharp-core-docs URL structure still matches — the current code generates URLs without `.html` extension (e.g., `.../fsharp-core-float` rather than `.../fsharp-core-float.html`). If the fsharp-core-docs GitHub Pages site requires the `.html` extension, that could cause broken links. A test covering FSharp.Core type links would also help ensure this stays working.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I investigated this issue and created a fix. Here's a summary of what I found:

**Root cause**: `ensureProjectWasRestored` has two problems:

1. A logic bug where the `try/with` block wraps both the `dotnet msbuild --getProperty:ProjectAssetsFile` call *and* the subsequent "definitely not restored" `failwithf` — so the latter is caught and replaced with the less-informative "Failed to detect..." message.
2. When the MSBuild call fails for any reason (old SDK without `--getProperty` support, non-standard project layout like dotnet/fsharp), it hard-fails rather than proceeding. This blocks `fsdocs` on any project that doesn't use the conventional `(projDir)/obj/` path.

**Fix**: Restructure the detection logic to distinguish:
- MSBuild succeeds + assets file exists → OK
- MSBuild succeeds + assets file missing → fail fast (project definitely not restored)
- MSBuild call itself fails → warn and proceed, letting the cracking step produce the definitive error

This directly follows your suggestion: *"make that failure a warning and let it fail hard later"*.

A draft PR has been opened with this fix.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I've investigated more closely and can now give a definitive answer: **the bug is NOT fixed**.

## Root Cause

I wrote and ran a diagnostic test using FCS directly to check what the API exposes for a C# class with `public readonly` fields:

**Test setup**: A C# class with `public readonly Guid Id` and `public readonly DateTimeOffset When`.

**FCS result via `FSharpEntity`**:
- `FSharpFields` count: **0** (should be 2)
- `MembersFunctionsAndValues` count: **1** (only the constructor — the fields are absent entirely)

I also tested regular (non-readonly) `public string MutableField` — same result: `FSharpFields` returns 0, the field is completely invisible.

## This is a FCS limitation

`FSharpEntity.FSharpFields` does not return C# class fields at all — neither `readonly` nor mutable. The FCS documentation says "from the perspective of the F# language", and from that perspective, C# fields simply aren't exposed through this property. They don't appear in `MembersFunctionsAndValues` either.

FSharp.Formatting's `readRecordFields` function (in `SymbolReader.fs`) uses `typ.FSharpFields`, so it will never see C# public fields through the current approach.

## Path to a Fix

A workaround within FSharp.Formatting (without requiring an FCS change) would be to supplement the FCS information with `System.Reflection`:

```fsharp
// For non-F# types (not IsFSharpRecord, not IsValueType enums):
// Use reflection to discover public instance fields that FCS misses
let reflectionFields = 
    let asm = System.Reflection.Assembly.LoadFrom(assemblyPath)
    match asm.GetType(typ.FullName) with
    | null -> [||]
    | t -> t.GetFields(BindingFlags.Public ||| BindingFlags.Instance)
```

Then the field names, types, and XML doc signatures (of the form `F:Namespace.Type.FieldName`) could be used to render them. This would require cross-referencing with the XML doc file for comments.

This would be a meaningful enhancement to `SymbolReader.fs` and `readRecordFields` (or a new `readCSharpFields` helper). Happy to attempt a PR if this direction seems right to the maintainers.

A 12-year-old request that would be genuinely useful! Here's an implementation sketch for anyone interested in picking this up.

**How it could work**

The `(*** include-toc ***)` directive (or `(** include-toc:2 **)` for depth 2) would be processed as a paragraph transformation pass after parsing, similar to how `(*** include-value:xxx ***)` works.

**Implementation approach**

1. **In `Literate.fs`** (the transformation pipeline): after parsing the document into a `LiterateDocument`, add a pass that:
   - Scans all `Heading(level, body, _)` nodes in the document AST
   - For each `(*** include-toc ***)` or `(*** include-toc:N ***)` directive, collects all headings up to depth N (default 3)
   - Generates anchors from the heading text (same anchor-generation logic already used by `generateAnchors`)
   - Returns a `ListBlock` of links (`DirectLink`) to those anchors

2. **In `HtmlFormatter.fs`**: the existing anchor-generation pass already adds `id=` attributes to headings (when `--generateAnchors` is set), so the links would work out of the box.

3. **New paragraph kind**: add `| InlineToc of depth: int` to the paragraph discriminated union in `MarkdownModel.fs`, or implement it as a special directive pattern in the existing `OtherBlock` handling.

**Simpler alternative**: process `(*** include-toc ***)` purely at string/HTML level in a final post-processing pass — scan for `<h2>`/`<h3>` tags and generate a `(nav)` block. Less elegant but avoids AST changes.

The `generateAnchors` path is key — without it, there are no `id=` attributes to link to. The feature would need to check (or require) `generateAnchors = true`.

Happy to prototype this if maintainers confirm the desired directive syntax and anchor strategy.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Looking at the current implementation in `CrossReferenceResolver`, this is still a valid enhancement. Here's what happens today and how tolerance could be added:

**Current behaviour**

`ResolveCref` in `GenerateModel.fs` handles:
- `T:Foo.Bar` → type lookup in `xmlDocNameToSymbol`
- `M:/P:/F:/E:` → member lookup
- `!:` → compiler unresolved warning
- Anything else → **"Unresolved reference" warning and silently discarded**

So `(see cref="Option"/)`, `(see cref="Option.Some"/)`, etc. all fall to the last case.

**Good news: the infrastructure is already partly there**

When entities are registered, the code already populates a `niceNameEntityLookup` dictionary (`Dictionary(string, List(FSharpEntity))`) that maps `LogicalName` (unqualified type name) → list of entities. This is at lines 824 and 867–870 of `GenerateModel.fs`.

**Proposed implementation approach**

The "No idea" fallback in `ResolveCref` could attempt:

1. **Unqualified type name**: if `cref` contains no `.` or dots only as separators, look it up in `niceNameEntityLookup`. If exactly one match → resolve to that entity's page. If multiple matches → pick by best namespace heuristic or link to the first.

2. **`Type.Member` pattern** (for constructors, methods): split on the last `.`, look up `Type` part in `niceNameEntityLookup`, then search `entity.MembersFunctionsAndValues` for the `Member` part by `DisplayName`.

3. **Short member name** (e.g. `(see cref="fold"/)` when writing within the same module): scan all registered members with matching display name.

The main challenges are ambiguity (many types named `Result`) and performance (full scan for short member names). A reasonable heuristic: if unambiguous, resolve; if ambiguous, take the first alphabetically and log a warning.

**Minimal first step**: just handle the `Type` and `Type.Member` patterns using `niceNameEntityLookup` — that covers the most common cases from the issue description. The short-name-only case is harder and could be deferred.

This is a self-contained improvement that wouldn't break existing well-formed crefs. Happy to take a stab at implementing this if the design approach looks good to maintainers.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This is a small but meaningful resource management improvement — dsyme confirmed this back in 2015, so I'm happy to provide the implementation details.

**Current state**: `FsiEvaluator` wraps an internal `FsiSession` (via `ScriptHost.Create` in `Evaluator.fs`, line 193). `FsiSession` already has a `Dispose()` method (line 1049 of `YaafFSharpScripting.fs`) that disposes the underlying `FSharp.Compiler.Interactive.Shell.FsiEvaluationSession`. However, `IFsiEvaluator` doesn't inherit `IDisposable`, and `FsiEvaluator` never calls `fsiSession.Dispose()` — so every `FsiEvaluator` instance leaks its F# interactive session.

**Proposed fix** — minimal change across two files:

1. **`src/FSharp.Formatting.Literate/Evaluator.fs`** — add `IDisposable` to the interface:
   ```fsharp
   type IFsiEvaluator =
       inherit IDisposable
       abstract Format: result: IFsiEvaluationResult * kind: FsiEmbedKind * executionCount: int -> MarkdownParagraphs
       abstract Evaluate: code: string * asExpression: bool * file: string option -> IFsiEvaluationResult
   ```

2. **`FsiEvaluator` class** — add `Dispose` to the `interface IFsiEvaluator with` block:
   ```fsharp
   member x.Dispose() = fsiSession.Dispose()
   ```

3. **`src/fsdocs-tool/BuildCommand.fs`** — wrap the evaluator in a `use` binding (line ~284):
   ```fsharp
   use evaluator = FsiEvaluator(onError = onError, options = [| "--multiemit-" |]) :> IFsiEvaluator
   ```

**Breaking change note**: Adding `inherit IDisposable` to `IFsiEvaluator` is technically a breaking change — any user who implements the interface directly will need to add a `Dispose()` method. In practice this is rare (most users only consume `FsiEvaluator`), but it should be noted in the release notes. An alternative that avoids the break is to leave `IFsiEvaluator` unchanged and just make `FsiEvaluator` implement `IDisposable` separately, but then callers can't dispose through the interface.

Happy to implement this if maintainers want to proceed — it's a very contained change.

This is a great feature request. Looking at the current code in `GenerateModel.fs`, the `getMembers` function already walks the base type chain, but only to *fold in* members from explicitly excluded/omitted base types — it doesn't collect inherited members for display:

```fsharp
let rec getMembers (typ: FSharpEntity) =
    [ yield! typ.MembersFunctionsAndValues
      match typ.BaseType with
      | Some baseType ->
          match cmds with
          | Command "exclude" _
          | Command "omit" _ -> yield! getMembers baseType.TypeDefinition
          | _ -> () // <-- inherited members from non-excluded bases are dropped
      | None -> () ]
```

To implement inherited member display (MSDN-style), the work would involve:

1. **Model changes**: Add an `InheritedMembers` field to `ApiDocEntity` that collects members from all base types in the chain, grouped by declaring type.

2. **Template changes**: Add an "Inherited Members" section in the HTML/Markdown templates (`partMembers.html` or similar) that renders these grouped by their source type with links to the base type's docs page.

3. **`ApiDocMember` attribution**: Each inherited member would need a reference back to its declaring `ApiDocEntity` so the template can render "Inherited from `BaseClass`".

The main challenge is that the members from base types (like `System.Object`) are typically not documented within FSharp.Formatting itself — the template would need to render cross-package links (e.g., to docs.microsoft.com for BCL types) in a meaningful way. It may be worth limiting inherited member display to types documented within the same assembly group initially.

`ApiDocEntity.BaseType` (line 604 in GenerateModel.fs) already exposes the base type with formatted HTML, so the foundation is there. A PR implementing step 1 and 2 above would be a good starting point.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.* This is a known CommonMark compliance gap in our Markdown list parser. The issue is that when a code block appears *inside* a list item (indented continuation), the parser incorrectly treats a leading `*` inside the fenced code block as a new sub-list item. **Root cause**: The list continuation logic in `MarkdownParser.fs` doesn't fully respect the CommonMark rule that fenced code blocks (``` ` ```-delimited) suppress normal inline/block parsing within them. When scanning for nested list items, the parser enters the indented content of the code block and sees `* Beta`, misidentifying it as the start of a new list item. **Relationship to ongoing work**: We've recently made some progress on CommonMark compliance — PR #1028 (now merged) improved ATX heading compliance. However, fixing list parsing is significantly more involved; the parser's list continuation and lookahead logic needs to be aware of whether it's currently inside a fenced code block. **Workaround**: Until this is fixed, you can work around it by using HTML code blocks (`
...
`) instead of fenced code blocks inside list items, which the parser handles differently. This is tracked as part of the broader #191 (CommonMark compliance) effort. A targeted fix would involve modifying the `parseParagraphLines`/`parseListItems` logic in `MarkdownParser.fs` to track fenced code block state during list parsing. > Generated by [Repo Assist](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/actions/runs/22452199872) > > To install this [agentic workflow](https://github.com/githubnext/agentics/tree/afb00b92a9514fee9a14c583f059a03d05738f70/workflows/repo-assist.md), run > ``` > gh aw add githubnext/agentics/workflows/repo-assist.md@afb00b92a9514fee9a14c583f059a03d05738f70 > ```

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

This bug was originally reported as an FCS limitation (FCS#235, 2015), but FCS has evolved significantly since then — let me share what I found in the current code.

**How member reading works today** (`SymbolReader.fs`, `readType` function):

```fsharp
let ivals, svals =
    typ.MembersFunctionsAndValues
    |> Seq.filter (fun v ->
        checkAccess ctx v.Accessibility
        && not v.IsCompilerGenerated
        && not v.IsOverrideOrExplicitInterfaceImplementation
        && not v.IsEventAddMethod
        && not v.IsEventRemoveMethod
        && not v.IsPropertyGetterMethod
        && not v.IsPropertySetterMethod)
    |> List.ofSeq
    |> List.partition (fun v -> v.IsInstanceMember)
```

**Potential culprit**: C# `public readonly` fields may or may not appear in `MembersFunctionsAndValues` in modern FCS, depending on whether FCS synthesizes property-like accessors for them. If they do appear, they should pass all filters above (they're not compiler-generated, not event/property accessors). If they don't appear at all, the bug is still in FCS's exposure of C# fields through that API.

**How to verify whether it's fixed**: Create a C# class with a `public readonly Guid Id` field, reference it from an F# project, and run `fsdocs build`. If the field appears in the generated API docs, the FCS bug has been fixed. If not, you'd need to check whether the field appears in `MembersFunctionsAndValues` at all.

Note: F# *record fields* are handled separately via `typ.FSharpFields`, not `MembersFunctionsAndValues` — so C# fields would only be exposed through the latter.

If someone can confirm this is still reproducible with the current version of FSharp.Formatting (and the FCS version it bundles), that would help prioritize a fix. A small reproducible test case would be very welcome!

Acting on `@nojaf`'s [feedback](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/pull/980#issuecomment-3949787104), I've pushed a commit that addresses both points:

---

### What changed

**1. `llms.txt` now links to `.md` files when markdown output is available**

`LlmsTxt.buildContent` accepts two new flags — `docContentUsesMarkdown` and `apiDocUsesMarkdown` — and transforms URIs accordingly:
- Doc content `.html` links → `.md` when `_template.md` is present in the input folder.
- API reference URIs (which have no extension in markdown mode, since `InUrl = ""`) get `.md` appended.

**2. Markdown output is generated by default when `FsDocsGenerateLlmsTxt` is enabled**

- `docs/_template.md` (the existing empty file) is now **bundled in the package** as `templates/_template.md`, so it's available even for projects that don't ship their own.
- When `FsDocsGenerateLlmsTxt = true` (the default), the API docs template selection **prefers `_template.md` over `_template.html`**, causing `GenerateMarkdownPhased` to be used and `.md` files to be written.
- `DocContent.Convert` accepts an optional `defaultMdTemplate` parameter; when `FsDocsGenerateLlmsTxt = true`, the bundled template is passed as the fallback so doc-content `.md` files are also generated.

---

### Potential concern to review

Preferring `_template.md` for API docs when `FsDocsGenerateLlmsTxt = true` means that for users who have **both** `_template.html` and `_template.md`, API docs will now be generated as markdown (`reference/type.md`) rather than HTML (`reference/type.html`). The HTML nav links in `_template.html` already reference the API pages using clean URLs (`InUrl = ""`), so they should still work — but this is worth verifying. If the behaviour change is undesirable, the ordering can be conditioned on the absence of `_template.html` rather than the presence of `_template.md`.

Nested navigation categories would be a great addition! Here's some context on the current design and a feasible implementation path.

**Current system**: Each `.md` / `.fsx` document can declare a `category` and `category-index` in its front matter. The sidebar groups documents by category in a flat list.

**Proposed approach** — use `/` as a separator in the `category` field:

```yaml
---
category: Collections/Lists
category-index: 2
---
```

This is minimally invasive (no new front-matter keys) and matches conventions in other static site generators. The nesting depth could be limited to two levels (parent/child) to keep the implementation manageable.

**What would need to change**:
1. Parse `category` values containing `/` to extract `(parentCategory, childCategory)`.
2. In the nav rendering code (`BuildCommand.fs`, around line 770–792), group by parent first, then emit child categories as indented nav items within each parent group.
3. The existing `category-index` would control ordering within each parent.

The sidebar HTML structure would use nested `<ul>` elements with a `nav-sub-header` CSS class for child groups.

If you're willing to work on this, it would be a very welcome PR — and the nav rendering code is reasonably approachable. Happy to help outline the data model changes if useful!

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

A fix has been implemented and a draft PR is being submitted. Here's a summary:

**Root cause confirmed**: In `MarkdownParser.fs`, the `LinesUntilListOrUnindented` active pattern stopped at *any* indented list start, regardless of depth. For a loose outer item like `"1.  List item"` (endIndent=4), the parser correctly stopped at `"    1. Subone"` — but then, when parsing the inner item `"    1. Subone"` (endIndent=7), it would also try to consume `"    Paragraph"` (4 leading spaces, which is *less* than endIndent=7 but *not* zero), absorbing it incorrectly into the first sublist item's body.

**Fix**: A new `LinesUntilListOrUnindentedLoose` pattern is used for loose list items (those preceded by a blank line). It does **not** stop at indented list starts — instead it captures all content at or above the item's indent level, stopping only at:
- Truly unindented content (0 leading spaces),
- Double blank lines, or
- A blank line followed by content whose effective indent is strictly between 0 and `endIndent` (under-indented content that belongs to an outer list item).

Tight list items (no blank line between marker and nested content) continue to use the original pattern, preserving all existing tight-list behaviour.

All 195 Markdown tests, 101 Literate tests, 69 ApiDocs tests and 20 CodeFormat tests pass. A regression test for this specific case has been added.

Parameters

**()**: `unit`

Example
...
(div class="fsdocs-tip" id="fs1")...(/div)
